result: I keep thinking about the hour just after the streetlights blink on, when the sky is still holding a bruised kind of light and the bats start flicking overhead like thrown confetti. It’s the only moment that feels secretly mine, even if half the city is out there breathing it too. Yesterday I stepped into that hour with groceries in twine-handled bags, the paper soft as an old map from the rain. A neighbor kid shot past on a bike, rear reflector spinning red planets against the curb, and for no reason he laughed back at me, a bright unguarded sound that left a small, clean hole in the fabric of whatever I’d been worrying about. I walked the rest of the block tasting that laugh, the way it mixed with the scent of wet lilacs and car exhaust, and I realized I’ve been collecting these almost-nothing moments the way other people collect stamps or grievances: the hiss of sprinkler water on hot pavement, the elderly cashier who calls me “young miss” though I’m neither, the pigeon that once landed on my café table and stared into my soul like a feathered therapist. String enough of them together and maybe they become a life raft, something buoyant to climb on when the big dark water rises. I’m not sure. But tonight I’ll be out there again, under the patient orange glow, pockets open, ears on, ready for the next scrap of ordinary magic to drift down.
